summary: The user, a ward councillor, conducted their first review of several municipal reports on solid waste management (SWM). Their primary focus was on citizen grievances. The user identified that Zones 10 and 11 had the highest complaint volumes and were both managed by a contractor named GreenHaul. By cross-referencing reports, the user discovered GreenHaul had a Grade 'F' performance rating, significant staff and vehicle shortfalls, and was the cause of paradoxical budget under-spending in its zones due to "service non-delivery." The user connected this contractor's failure to severe consequences, including extremely long complaint resolution times, low waste collection efficiency, and direct financial losses for the city, as emergency hire costs exceeded penalties collected. They also explored the link between poor SWM and public health crises. While acknowledging a proposed Waste-to-Energy plant as a long-term solution, the user concluded that the most urgent, actionable problem was the systemic failure of GreenHaul, and felt confident in raising this issue with the committee.
key_facts: Total SWM complaints reached 22,660, a 28.4% increase year-over-year.
Zones 10 and 11, operated by contractor GreenHaul, account for the highest number of complaints.
GreenHaul received a Grade 'F' performance rating (40.5/100) and has a 34% staff shortfall.
The city spent INR 2.4 crore on emergency fleet hire to cover GreenHaul's shortfall, while collecting only INR 1.03 crore in penalties from them.
Average complaint resolution time in GreenHaul's zones is 68.2 hours, with only 26% resolved within the 48-hour target.
Waste collection efficiency in GreenHaul's zones is only 58%, dragging down the city-wide average.
A report recommends initiating contract termination proceedings against GreenHaul.
The city's landfills have an estimated 28 months of capacity remaining, while a proposed Waste-to-Energy plant solution would take over 5 years to build.
The Public Health report estimates an annual economic burden of INR 48.6 crore due to health issues linked to waste sites.
The city's overall cost recovery for SWM services is only 5.3%.
positions_taken: The user believes the situation in Zones 10 and 11 is a 'disaster' for residents.
The user concluded that contractor failure, poor service, health crises, and financial costs form a 'vicious cycle'.
The user identified the failure of the contractor GreenHaul as the most urgent operational issue to be addressed.
The user expressed that the situation with GreenHaul constitutes a 'huge reputational risk' for the city.
The user believes the proposed Waste-to-Energy plant is the 'only real long-term solution'.
The user feels confident in their evidence-based understanding of the GreenHaul problem and is prepared to raise it in committee.
